module.title: Compression
module.description: Go beyond the basics — understand compressor types and their characters, shape transients, use parallel compression, glue buses, and train your ear to hear what compression is actually doing.

lessons.title: Compression Types and Characters
lessons.body:
Not all compressors sound the same. Different compressor circuit designs — VCA, FET, optical, variable-mu, and digital — each have distinct characteristics that affect how they respond to transients, how much harmonic colour they add, and how natural or aggressive the compression sounds. Knowing these characters helps you choose the right tool for each job.

## Why Compressor Types Differ

In the analogue era, compression was achieved through different circuit topologies, each with its own electrical behaviour and timing imprecision. Those imprecisions became the sound. Digital compressors model these behaviours — some add the harmonic colouration and non-linear response of the original hardware, others are transparent and clinical.

FL Studio's Fruity Limiter is a clean digital compressor — excellent for control, minimal in colour. Third-party compressor plugins often model specific hardware types for colour and character.

## VCA Compressors

**Character**: Fast, punchy, versatile. Minimal colouration. Precise and predictable.

**Behaviour**: VCA (voltage-controlled amplifier) compressors respond very quickly and consistently. They can have extremely fast attack and release times, making them excellent for controlling transients precisely.

**Sound**: Clean with a slight hardness. The compression is obvious when pushed but not particularly warm or musical.

**Typical uses**: Drums, bass, bus compression, anywhere you want tight control without added colour. The SSL G-Bus compressor — a famous VCA design — is the most iconic bus compressor in pop and hip-hop production.

**FL Studio equivalent**: Fruity Limiter in COMP mode behaves most like a clean VCA — fast, controllable, transparent.

## FET Compressors

**Character**: Aggressive, fast, forward. Adds harmonic saturation at higher compression amounts.

**Behaviour**: FET (field-effect transistor) compressors are even faster than VCA in some designs. They impart a distinctive forward, punchy quality and add upper harmonic content at higher gain reduction levels.

**Sound**: Punchy and slightly aggressive. Higher ratios and fast settings add a pleasant saturation character — the compression becomes part of the tone, not just control.

**Typical uses**: Snares, lead vocals, electric bass, anything that benefits from added presence and attack. The UA 1176 is the archetypal FET compressor.

**Practical note**: When using a plugin modelling a FET compressor, push the input gain higher than you normally would — the saturation and character emerge at higher drive levels.

## Optical Compressors

**Character**: Smooth, natural, slow. Transparent at low to moderate settings.

**Behaviour**: Optical compressors use a light source and a photocell to detect and respond to the signal. The photocell has an inherent, non-linear release time — it recovers slowly at first, then faster — which sounds extremely natural and musical.

**Sound**: Gentle and transparent. The compression feels like the sound is breathing rather than being controlled. Hard to hear as compression; easy to hear as a better-sounding source.

**Typical uses**: Vocals, acoustic instruments, bass, mix buses. The LA-2A is the iconic optical compressor — used on almost every major record of the past 50 years for its musical, hands-off character.

**In FL Studio**: There is no native optical emulation; this is where third-party plugins earn their cost for vocal production.

## Variable-Mu Compressors

**Character**: Warm, slow, and wide. The most musical-sounding compressor type for full-mix use.

**Behaviour**: Variable-mu (also called tube or vari-mu) compressors use vacuum tubes whose gain changes with the input level. They are inherently slow — they cannot catch fast transients — and add significant harmonic warmth.

**Sound**: Thick, warm, gluing. Sounds like the music was recorded together in one room. Transients pass through relatively untouched; sustained content gets compressed gently.

**Typical uses**: Master bus, mix bus, drum room mics. The Fairchild 670 and Manley Vari-Mu are the famous examples. A favourite for adding warmth and cohesion to the full mix without obvious compression.

## Digital / Linear Phase Compressors

**Character**: Transparent and surgical. Exactly what you set is exactly what happens.

**Behaviour**: Digital compressors introduce minimal colouration and respond with perfect accuracy to their parameter settings. Some add zero-latency lookahead (they can react before the transient using buffered processing).

**Sound**: Clean and precise. Can sound clinical or flat if overused.

**Typical uses**: Mastering, podcast production, anywhere transparency matters more than colour. Fruity Limiter is this type.

## Choosing by Task

| Task | Character to Reach For |
|---|---|
| Drum transient control | VCA (fast, precise) |
| Snare punch and bite | FET (aggressive, forward) |
| Vocal smoothing | Optical (natural, transparent) |
| Full mix warmth and glue | Variable-mu (slow, warm) |
| Master bus peak control | Digital / Limiter (transparent) |
| Bass consistency | VCA or optical |

In FL Studio's stock plugin set, Fruity Limiter covers the VCA/digital territory well. For optical and FET characters, budget plugins like TDR Kotelnikov (transparent bus compressor) or Molot (warm VCA-ish character) are free and high quality.
keyTakeaways: VCA compressors are fast and precise with minimal colour — ideal for drums and bus control. FET compressors add aggressive punch and harmonic saturation — ideal for snares and leads. Optical compressors are smooth and natural — ideal for vocals and bass.
Variable-mu compressors are too slow to catch transients but add warmth and glue across sustained content — they are the classic full-mix bus and master bus tool for cohesion.

Fruity Limiter behaves like a clean digital/VCA compressor — excellent for control and transparency. For optical warmth or FET aggression on vocals or individual sources, a third-party plugin is worth the investment.
lessons.tryThis: Open two instances of Fruity Limiter on your snare track. Set both to 4:1 ratio and −18 dB threshold. On the first, set attack to 1 ms (fast — catching the transient immediately). On the second, set attack to 50 ms (slow — letting the transient through). Bypass one at a time and listen to how fast attack dulls the snap of the snare while slow attack preserves it. This attack timing behaviour is what separates VCA-style control from optical-style naturalness — the same compressor, two different characters based on one parameter.

lessons.title: Transient Shaping
lessons.body:
Every sound has two parts: the attack (the initial transient — the sharp spike at the start of the note) and the sustain (everything that follows). Transient shaping is the direct manipulation of these two stages — independently of dynamics or volume. It lets you make a kick punch harder without making it louder, or make a snare tail longer without adjusting compression.

## Why Transients Matter

Transients are what the brain uses to identify and locate sounds. A sharp transient makes a drum sound tight and close — you hear it as a hit. A soft transient makes it sound distant and roomy. Too much transient on every element creates a harsh, exhausting mix. Too little transient makes a mix feel dull and distant.

Most mixing problems described as 'lacking punch' or 'sounding muddy' are transient problems — the attacks are too soft, too consistent, or too buried under sustain.

## Transient Shaping vs Compression

Compression controls dynamic range by turning the level down when the signal exceeds a threshold. It affects transients as a side effect of that level control.

Transient shaping works differently — it directly increases or decreases the attack portion and the sustain portion of a sound, without reference to level thresholds. A transient shaper asks: *do you want more or less attack? more or less sustain?* and applies that directly.

| Tool | Mechanism | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| Compressor | Threshold-based level reduction | Dynamic control, glue |
| Transient shaper | Direct attack/sustain manipulation | Punch, tightness, room control |

## Fruity Peak Controller as a Transient Envelope

FL Studio does not include a dedicated transient shaper plugin. The closest native approach uses **Fruity Peak Controller** or **envelope follower** techniques — or you can achieve similar results with compression attack/release settings:

**Adding attack (more punch)**:
- Use a compressor with a slow attack (40–80 ms) so the transient passes through uncompressed before gain reduction kicks in. The transient is preserved; the sustain is reduced. Result: more perceived punch.

**Reducing attack (softening transients)**:
- Use a compressor with a very fast attack (1–5 ms). The compressor catches the transient immediately and reduces it. This is how to tame a snare that is too aggressive or a hi-hat that is too clickey.

**Adding sustain (longer tail)**:
- Slow release on a compressor allows the sustain portion to stay at a higher level after the transient peak subsides. The tail feels longer and fuller.

**Reducing sustain (tighter sound)**:
- Fast release on a compressor lets the gain return to normal quickly. Combined with a moderate ratio, this tightens the sound — each hit feels discrete and punchy rather than smearing into the next.

## Transient Shaping with Third-Party Plugins

Dedicated transient shapers (Waves Smack Attack, Slate Digital Fresh Air, TDR Kotelnikov's transient response, or the free MSED/Transient Master equivalents) give you direct attack and sustain knobs:

- **Attack knob positive**: Boosts the initial transient — more punch and click
- **Attack knob negative**: Reduces the transient — softer, more distant hit
- **Sustain knob positive**: Extends the decay — more room, more body
- **Sustain knob negative**: Cuts the sustain — tighter, drier sound

## Practical Transient Decisions by Instrument

**Kick drum**:
- More attack: the click at 3–5 kHz is what cuts through on small speakers — if the kick disappears in the mix, add attack
- Less sustain: a boomy kick with too much sustain clutters the bass range — reduce sustain to tighten

**Snare**:
- More attack: makes the snap and crack more prominent — useful if the snare feels buried
- Less attack: tames an overly aggressive or sharp snare that causes fatigue
- More sustain: adds a longer snare ring that sounds more live and roomy

**Hi-hats and cymbals**:
- Less attack: removes the harsh initial tick from a metallic hi-hat sample
- Less sustain: reduces the wash and bleed between hits — tighter, more defined patterns

**Bass / 808**:
- More attack: emphasises the initial pluck or sub hit — more defined note starts
- Less sustain: tightens a sub that rings too long after each note, creating pitch clashes

## The Timing Relationship

Transient shaping interacts with the tempo and feel of the track. Short attacks and short sustains feel tight and modern (trap, electronic music). Long sustains and softer attacks feel loose and organic (lo-fi, jazz, soul). Neither is correct — choose based on the genre feel you are building.
keyTakeaways: Slow compressor attack (40–80 ms) lets the transient pass through before gain reduction engages — this preserves punch. Fast attack (1–5 ms) catches and reduces the transient — this softens a too-aggressive hit.
Most 'lacking punch' mix problems are transient problems — the attacks are buried under sustain or competing with other transients. Address the transient directly before reaching for volume or EQ.

Fast release tightens a sound by letting the compressor recover quickly between hits; slow release extends the sustain tail by keeping the gain reduced longer — use these intentionally to control the feel of rhythmic elements.
lessons.tryThis: On your kick drum, add Fruity Limiter in COMP mode. Set ratio to 4:1, threshold to −18 dB. Now try two extreme attack settings back to back: set attack to 1 ms and listen — the kick loses its initial punch and sounds dull. Then set attack to 60 ms and listen — the transient snaps through before compression engages and the kick hits harder. Settle on the attack time that gives you the punch you want, then adjust release to control how tight or sustained the kick body feels. Toggle the plugin on and off at each setting to confirm what the compressor is actually contributing.
